title: Listening - Part 3: Receiving a Present
description: You are going to hear five extracts in which people talk about a present they received. For questions 19–23, choose from the list A–H why each speaker particularly valued the present. Use the letters once only. There are three letters that you do not need to use.
instructions: You are going to hear five extracts in which people talk about a present they received. For questions 19–23, choose from the list A–H why each speaker particularly valued the present. Use the letters once only. There are three letters that you do not need to use.

extracts.context: Five people talk about a present they received.
extracts.audio_script: Speaker 1 Woman: For my birthday, my friend gave me a knitted scarf. It isn't in colours I would normally choose, and it has one slightly uneven corner. But she had recently learned to knit, despite having a very busy job. [19]When I realised she had spent several evenings creating it herself rather than simply buying something, the scarf immediately became far more valuable to me than any perfect item from a shop.[/19] I wear it constantly in winter, and the uneven corner just makes me smile.||Speaker 2 Man: My uncle gave me a small telescope when I was twelve. At first I thought it might be one of those presents that remains in a cupboard after the first week. Then he showed me how to point it at the moon. [20]That gift led me to start observing the night sky, reading about planets and eventually joining an astronomy club, an interest I would probably never have discovered otherwise.[/20] I now have better equipment, but I still keep the original telescope in my room.||Speaker 3 Woman: My family usually gives me practical birthday gifts, such as clothes or money, which are useful but not very personal. Last year my brother gave me a subscription to a magazine about railway journeys. [21]I'd only mentioned once that I love planning train trips and studying old railway maps, so his choice made me realise he had really listened and understood what genuinely interests me.[/21] The magazine itself is enjoyable, but I think it was the thought behind it that made me happiest.||Speaker 4 Man: My grandmother died several years ago, and she had always worn a simple silver watch. When I finished university, my mother gave it to me after having it repaired. It isn't fashionable and it gains a few minutes each week. [22]I was deeply touched because it was an object my grandmother had used every day, and wearing it makes me feel connected to her in a way a new watch never could.[/22] I only wear it on special occasions because I'm afraid of damaging it.||Speaker 5 Woman: After finishing school, three friends and I travelled around Greece together. We had a wonderful time, but once we returned, we were all busy and met less frequently. At Christmas one friend gave each of us a small photo book. [23]It contained pictures, ticket stubs and funny comments from that holiday, so turning its pages brought back moments I had almost forgotten from a journey that meant a great deal to us.[/23] Whenever we meet now, someone usually mentions a story from the book.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_1
dialogue.text: For my birthday, my friend gave me a knitted scarf. It isn't in colours I would normally choose, and it has one slightly uneven corner. But she had recently learned to knit, despite having a very busy job. When I realised she had spent several evenings creating it herself rather than simply buying something, the scarf immediately became far more valuable to me than any perfect item from a shop. I wear it constantly in winter, and the uneven corner just makes me smile.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_2
dialogue.text: My uncle gave me a small telescope when I was twelve. At first I thought it might be one of those presents that remains in a cupboard after the first week. Then he showed me how to point it at the moon. That gift led me to start observing the night sky, reading about planets and eventually joining an astronomy club, an interest I would probably never have discovered otherwise. I now have better equipment, but I still keep the original telescope in my room.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_3
dialogue.text: My family usually gives me practical birthday gifts, such as clothes or money, which are useful but not very personal. Last year my brother gave me a subscription to a magazine about railway journeys. I'd only mentioned once that I love planning train trips and studying old railway maps, so his choice made me realise he had really listened and understood what genuinely interests me. The magazine itself is enjoyable, but I think it was the thought behind it that made me happiest.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_4
dialogue.text: My grandmother died several years ago, and she had always worn a simple silver watch. When I finished university, my mother gave it to me after having it repaired. It isn't fashionable and it gains a few minutes each week. I was deeply touched because it was an object my grandmother had used every day, and wearing it makes me feel connected to her in a way a new watch never could. I only wear it on special occasions because I'm afraid of damaging it.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_5
dialogue.text: After finishing school, three friends and I travelled around Greece together. We had a wonderful time, but once we returned, we were all busy and met less frequently. At Christmas one friend gave each of us a small photo book. It contained pictures, ticket stubs and funny comments from that holiday, so turning its pages brought back moments I had almost forgotten from a journey that meant a great deal to us. Whenever we meet now, someone usually mentions a story from the book.

options.text: It enabled them to start a new interest.
It had been made by the giver.
It reminded them of a special trip.
It replaced something they had lost.
It was something they needed for work.
It showed that the giver understood them.
It had belonged to an older relative.
It arrived when they were feeling unhappy.

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: The speaker values the scarf because the friend spent time making it herself (evidence [19]).

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: Receiving the telescope introduced the speaker to a lasting interest in astronomy (evidence [20]).

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: The gift mattered because it demonstrated that the brother knew and remembered the speaker's unusual interest (evidence [21]).

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: The speaker values the watch because it once belonged to her grandmother (evidence [22]).

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: The photo book is appreciated because it recalls an important shared holiday (evidence [23]).
